title: Photon correlation microscopy of quantum matter
authors: Elie Vandoolaeghe, Iñigo Lasheras, Chirag Vaswani, Sampriti Saha, Purbasha Ray, Takashi Taniguchi, Kenji Watanabe, Prasana Sahoo, Nicolò Defenu, Thibault Chervy, Puneet A. Murthy
abstract: Light and matter share fundamental statistical properties, yet the experimental probes of quantum optics and many-body physics have largely evolved along separate trajectories. While many-body physics explores emergent collective phenomena, quantum optics has refined the measurement of correlations between individual photons. Here, we introduce photon correlation microscopy (PCM) - which bridges the two domains by leveraging correlations of emitted light to probe the correlations in quantum matter at mesoscopic scales. We demonstrate this approach using a one-dimensional (1D) ensemble of dipolar excitons confined at a lateral monolayer MoSe$_2$-WSe$_2$ heterojunction. We use gate-defined potentials to confine the 1D excitons to a mesoscopic lengthscale to enhance the visibility of matter correlations in the emitted photon field. Power-dependent spectroscopy reveals a transition from a compressible to an incompressible phase, signaled by the simultaneous saturation of the emission intensity and energy blueshift, which is supported by numerical simulations. Through this crossover, photon correlation measurements show a striking evolution from bunching at low densities to antibunching at high densities. This constitutes a many-body blockade of photon emission emerging directly from a number-stabilized state, driven by collective dipolar repulsion. Our results establish PCM as a powerful probe of many-body physics through the lens of quantum optics, extensible to a broad class of correlated electronic phases, while pointing toward a route to generating non-classical light through many-body correlations.
summary: This paper introduces Photon Correlation Microscopy to study many-body correlations in 1D dipolar excitons. By measuring photon statistics, the authors observe a transition from photon bunching to antibunching, signaling a crossover from a compressible thermal gas to an incompressible, number-stabilized state. This technique provides a new way to probe correlated electronic phases using optical tools.
main_problem: Bridging the gap between quantum optics and many-body physics by developing a method to probe real-space many-body correlations and compressibility in quantum matter at mesoscopic scales.
main_result: The authors demonstrate Photon Correlation Microscopy (PCM) to observe a density-driven crossover in a 1D dipolar exciton gas from a compressible thermal phase (photon bunching) to an incompressible, number-stabilized quasi-crystal phase (photon antibunching).
method: The study combines experimental Hanbury Brown-Twiss interferometry and power-dependent spectroscopy with numerical many-body simulations using the Multi-Configuration Time-Dependent Hartree method for bosons (MCTDH-B).
model_or_system: A 1D ensemble of dipolar excitons confined in mesoscopic, gate-defined traps at a lateral MoSe2-WSe2 heterojunction, characterized by large permanent in-plane electric dipole moments and long radiative lifetimes.
key_observables: Second-order photon correlation function g2(0), photon bunching/antibunching, isothermal compressibility, particle number fluctuations, and photon recovery time.
important_parameters: Trap widths (50, 100, 150 nm), pump power, dipolar interaction strength, and the ratio of sampling length to coherence length.
main_assumptions: The system is assumed to be in quasi-equilibrium (thermalization time is much shorter than radiative lifetime), and the photon statistics are assumed to track the local density fluctuations.
figures_summary: Figure 1 provides a schematic of PCM and the transition from thermal to incompressible phases; Figure 4 shows power-dependent photon correlation measurements for different trap sizes; Supplemental Figure S6 shows chemical potential and density distributions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the PCM technique, describes the experimental platform and confinement method, presents the observation of the phase crossover via photon statistics, provides a theoretical validation of the link between photon and matter correlations, and concludes with scaling and dynamical analysis.
why_interesting: It establishes a powerful new bridge between quantum optics and many-body physics, showing how photon statistics can serve as a non-invasive probe for complex phases like Wigner crystals or Mott insulators in semiconductor systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Photon correlation microscopy of many-body systems. (a) Matter. A mesoscopic many-body system of emitters exhibiting three distinct phases: a compressible thermal phase (yellow), a quantum-degenerate phase (purple), and a strongly correlated, incompressible phase (blue). These are mirrored in three complementary observables (left to right): the number distribution p(N) broadens in the thermal phase and sharpens toward the incompressible regime; the matter pair correlator g(2) mat(r) evolves (for bosons) from spatial bunching to a deepening correlation hole; the compressibility κT collapses as repulsive interactions stiffen the system. (b) Light. Emitted photons are sent into a...

FIG. 2. Mesoscopic confinement of dipolar excitons. (a) Schematic of the dual-gated device: a lateral MoSe2–WSe2 semiconductor heterostructure encapsulated in hBN. Nanostructured top-gate fingers of width L = 50, 100, 150 nm, oriented perpendicular to the lateral interface, define confinement regions for interfacial dipolar excitons (inset). (b) Micrograph of the device with top-gate fingers (yellow) and the lateral interface (red) outlined; inset: SEM image of the top-gate fingers. (c) Simulated longitudinal trapping potential for L ≈50 nm shows flat-bottomed 1D confinement. (d) VBG-dependent PL spectra at VTG = 0. The unconfined interface exciton XLI exhibits a characteristic blueshift...

FIG. 3. Interaction-driven crossover. (a, b) Power-dependent PL spectra (normalized by integration time) for the L = 50 nm and L = 100 nm traps, respectively. As the power is increased, emission shifts sequentially from the lowest transverse subband to higher-lying ones. (c) Integrated PL count rate (normalized by the average photon energy) for the L = 50 nm (orange), L = 100 nm (blue) and L = 150 nm (green) traps, displaying a steep rise followed by saturation at higher powers. (d) Mean emission energy for the same traps, exhibiting a similar steep rise and saturation. The integrated counts and the mean energy saturate at the same power Psat for each trap, with Psat depending on the trap...
FIG. 4. Many-body blockade of photon emission. (a, b) Power-dependent g(2) ph (τ) for the L = 50 nm and 100 nm traps.
